q: What is Azure AI Foundry (now branded Microsoft Foundry), and how does it relate to Azure OpenAI?
a: Azure AI Foundry is the unified Azure platform-as-a-service for building, deploying, and governing GenAI applications — it consolidates the model catalog, Azure OpenAI, agents, evaluations, content safety, and connections behind one portal and SDK. Azure OpenAI is one service inside Foundry (the GPT/embeddings/o-series models), not the whole platform. Foundry adds a project layer for organizing assets, RBAC, and shared infrastructure on top of the raw model APIs. Microsoft rebranded 'Azure AI Foundry' to 'Microsoft Foundry' in late 2025, and the older hub-based experience is now called 'Foundry (classic).'
fj: Azure AI Foundry and Azure OpenAI are the same product with two names.
fs: Foundry is the platform; Azure OpenAI is one model service inside it, alongside Search, agents, eval, and content safety.

followups.q: Besides Azure OpenAI, what other model providers can you reach through the Foundry model catalog?
followups.a: The catalog spans OpenAI plus open and partner models — Meta `Llama`, Mistral, Cohere, DeepSeek, Microsoft's own `Phi`, and others — exposed either as serverless pay-per-token APIs or as managed-compute deployments. This lets you standardize one SDK and RBAC surface while picking the best model per task, and swap providers without re-plumbing the app. The point of Foundry is provider-agnostic access, not OpenAI lock-in.

followups.q: What does the unified Foundry SDK give you over installing the `openai` Python package directly?
followups.a: The `azure-ai-projects` client gives one authenticated handle to a project from which you reach agents, evaluations, datasets, connections, and the inference endpoint, all under Entra identity and project RBAC. You still get an OpenAI-shaped chat client for raw inference, but routed through the project so secrets, connections, and telemetry are centralized. So it's a superset: project orchestration plus the familiar inference call.

followups.q: If 'Foundry (classic)' is the old experience, what should a team building today actually target?
followups.a: Build on the new Foundry resource and project model and the current `azure-ai-projects` / Foundry Agent Service path, not the classic hub-based portal or the deprecated Assistants API. Classic still works for existing workloads, but Microsoft is steering new agent and orchestration work to the Agent Framework and Responses-API-based Agent Service. Targeting classic now means migrating again soon.

q: In Azure AI Foundry, what is the difference between a hub and a project, and why does that structure exist?
a: In the classic experience a hub is the top-level resource holding shared infrastructure — networking, customer-managed keys, managed identity, connections, and compute quota — while projects are workspaces under a hub where teams build flows, deploy models, and store assets like agents and evaluations. The hub lets a platform team centralize security and connectivity once, and many project teams inherit it without re-plumbing. Policy and quota set on the hub apply to every project beneath it. The newer Foundry portal flattens this into a Foundry resource with lighter project containers, but the hub/project mental model is still common in interviews.
fj: Each project is fully independent and configures its own networking and keys.
fs: Hub = shared infra/security/quota; projects inherit it and just hold build assets. Centralize once, build many.

followups.q: What is a 'connection' in this model and why isn't it just a stored API key?
followups.a: A connection is a managed, RBAC-governed link from the hub/project to an external resource — Azure AI Search, Storage, another AOAI resource, or a third-party endpoint — that can authenticate via Entra managed identity rather than a raw key. Defining it once on the hub lets every project consume the dependency without each team copying secrets. It centralizes credential rotation and access auditing, which a bare key in app settings can't.

followups.q: How does RBAC differ between hub-level and project-level roles?
followups.a: Hub-level roles (e.g. owner/contributor on the hub) govern shared infra, connections, and which teams can create projects, while project-level roles scope a user to building inside one workspace — deploying models, running evals, editing agents — without touching shared networking or other projects' assets. This separates the platform team from app teams. Granting project access doesn't grant hub control, which is the whole point of the split.

followups.q: If the new Foundry resource flattens hubs away, how do teams still share infrastructure?
followups.a: In the new model the Foundry account is the parent resource and projects are lightweight containers under it, so shared identity, networking (managed VNet/private endpoints), and connections attach at the account level and projects inherit them. You still get 'configure once, build many' — the hierarchy is just simpler, and there's a migration story from classic hubs. The governance intent is unchanged; the resource shape is lighter.

q: When should a team use Azure AI Foundry versus calling the raw Azure OpenAI REST/SDK directly?
a: Raw Azure OpenAI is fine for a single deployment hit by a simple app: you get the chat/embeddings endpoints, content filtering, and per-token billing with minimal setup. Reach for Foundry once you need more than inference — a model catalog beyond OpenAI, managed agents, prompt/eval tooling, connections to Azure AI Search for RAG, groundedness and safety evaluations, and centralized RBAC/networking across teams. Foundry doesn't replace the OpenAI API; it wraps and orchestrates around it. The decision driver is whether you're shipping one endpoint or operating a GenAI portfolio with governance.
fj: Always use Foundry — the raw Azure OpenAI API is deprecated.
fs: Raw API for one simple deployment; Foundry when you need catalog, agents, eval, RAG wiring, and org-wide governance.

followups.q: Does adopting Foundry force you to rewrite existing raw Azure OpenAI calls?
followups.a: No — Foundry wraps the same model endpoints, so your `openai`-style chat/embeddings calls keep working; you point them at the project's inference endpoint and authenticate with Entra instead of a key. You adopt the orchestration, eval, and agent features incrementally on top. There's no forced rewrite, which makes Foundry an additive layer rather than a migration.

followups.q: What governance gap does raw-API usage at scale create that pushes teams to Foundry?
followups.a: With many teams each calling raw AOAI resources you lose central visibility: scattered keys, no shared eval baseline, inconsistent content-safety config, and no unified RBAC or audit. Foundry consolidates connections, identity, evaluations, and telemetry so a platform team can enforce policy once. The failure mode it prevents is dozens of ungoverned, unmonitored endpoints sprawling across subscriptions.

followups.q: Is there latency or cost overhead from going through Foundry instead of the raw endpoint?
followups.a: Inference billing is the same per-token model whether you call AOAI directly or through a Foundry project — Foundry doesn't add a token surcharge. The orchestration features (agents, eval runs, managed retrieval) have their own costs, and managed compute or AI Search bill separately, but a plain chat call routed through the project endpoint is effectively the same request. Overhead comes from features you opt into, not the wrapper.

q: How is Azure OpenAI capacity rate-limited? Explain TPM, RPM, and how quota maps to deployments.
a: Quota is granted per subscription, per region, per model as Tokens-Per-Minute (TPM); you allocate that TPM across one or more deployments in increments of 1,000. Each deployment's TPM sets its token rate limit, and an RPM (Requests-Per-Minute) limit is derived proportionally from the TPM, though the RPM:TPM ratio varies by model. So a 240K TPM regional grant for a model can be one 240K deployment or several smaller ones that sum under 240K. Exceeding either limit returns HTTP 429, so you handle it with ret/backoff or spread load across regions.
fj: Each deployment gets unlimited throughput and quota only caps how many deployments you can create.
fs: TPM is a per-region/per-model budget you split across deployments; RPM is derived from TPM; overflow = 429, so back off or spread regions.

code.label: create a capped deployment

followups.q: How should a client correctly react to a 429 beyond just retrying immediately?
followups.a: Honor the `Retry-After` header AOAI returns on a 429 and back off for that duration rather than hammering, ideally with exponential backoff and jitter on top. Retrying instantly just burns more quota and deepens the throttle. For sustained overflow, the real fix is more TPM, spreading across deployments/regions, or moving to provisioned throughput — retries only smooth transient spikes.

followups.q: How do Standard, Global Standard, and Provisioned (PTU) deployment types change the rate-limit story?
followups.a: Standard and Global/Data-Zone Standard are pay-per-token with shared TPM-based limits and 429s under contention, where Global pools capacity across regions for higher availability. Provisioned (PTUs) instead reserve dedicated capacity measured in throughput units with predictable latency and no token-rate 429s in the same sense — you're capped by your provisioned units, not shared quota. So PTUs trade pay-as-you-go for reserved, steadier throughput.

followups.q: Why does spreading the same model across two regions improve resilience, and what's the catch?
followups.a: Quota is per-region, so a second region gives you an independent TPM budget and a failover target if one region throttles or has an outage — you load-balance and double effective capacity. The catch is data residency (traffic now lands in another geography), added routing complexity, and per-region model/version availability differences. Global Standard deployments achieve similar capacity pooling without you managing regions manually.

q: Why do Azure OpenAI deployments pin a specific model version, and what is the risk of auto-update?
a: A deployment binds a model name to a dated version (e.g. gpt-4o-2024-11-20) so your app's behavior is reproducible; prompts and evals tuned against one version can shift if the weights change. Azure offers update policies — pin to a version, or auto-update to default — and retires old versions on a published schedule. Auto-update keeps you current but can silently change output formatting, refusal behavior, or token counts, breaking parsers or regressing eval scores. Senior practice is to pin in production, test new versions in a staging deployment against your eval set, then promote deliberately.
fj: Always enable auto-update so you never fall behind on model quality.
fs: Pin versions in prod for reproducibility; auto-update can silently change outputs and break evals/parsers. Promote via staging.

followups.q: If you pin a version, what forces you to act eventually, and how do you find out?
followups.a: Pinned versions still get retired on Azure's published model-retirement schedule, after which the deployment must move to a newer version. Azure surfaces retirement dates in the portal/docs and sends advance notifications, and a pinned deployment past its retirement date can be auto-upgraded to the default. So pinning buys reproducibility but not immortality — you must track the retirement calendar and migrate ahead of it.

followups.q: How does a regression-test gate for a model upgrade actually look in practice?
followups.a: You keep a labeled eval set and run it through a staging deployment on the candidate version, scoring groundedness, format compliance, refusal behavior, and task accuracy against the current production version as a baseline. Only if the candidate matches or beats baseline do you promote. Foundry's evaluation tooling (or prompt-flow-style eval runs, now Agent Framework) automates this comparison so promotion is data-driven, not vibes.

followups.q: Beyond the model version, what else can change a deployment's behavior without your code changing?
followups.a: Content-filter / safety configuration changes, default `system` prompt scaffolding, API version (`api-version`) differences, and tokenizer or default-parameter shifts can all move outputs even on a pinned model. So reproducibility means pinning the model version, the API version, and your own parameters/prompt together. Treating only the model version as fixed leaves other moving parts that can still cause drift.

q: What does Azure AI Search contribute to a RAG system, and what retrieval modes does it offer?
a: Azure AI Search is the retrieval layer: you index your chunked content with both text and vector fields, then query to fetch the passages a model grounds its answer on. It supports keyword (BM25), pure vector (HNSW/eKNN over embeddings), and hybrid — which runs both and fuses results with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). On top of that, the semantic ranker re-scores the top results with a Microsoft language model for relevance. Hybrid plus semantic ranking is Microsoft's recommended 'classic RAG' default because it beats either signal alone on relevance benchmarks.
fj: Vector search alone always beats keyword search, so hybrid is unnecessary.
fs: AI Search = the RAG retriever; hybrid (BM25 + vector via RRF) plus a semantic reranker is the recommended high-relevance default.

code.label: hybrid + semantic query

followups.q: Why does HNSW use an approximate nearest-neighbor search instead of exact, and when would you choose exhaustive KNN?
followups.a: HNSW builds a navigable graph so vector search runs in sub-linear time at large scale, trading a tiny recall loss for big latency/cost wins — essential for millions of vectors. Exhaustive (`exhaustiveKnn`) compares the query against every vector for exact results, which you reserve for small indexes or for ground-truth evaluation of recall. You can even flag a single query as exhaustive over an HNSW index when you need an exact pass.

followups.q: RRF fuses ranks, not raw scores — why does that matter for combining BM25 and vector results?
followups.a: BM25 scores and cosine/vector similarities live on incomparable scales, so naively adding them lets one signal dominate. RRF instead sums reciprocal-rank contributions (`1/(k+rank)`) from each result list, normalizing by position rather than magnitude, which makes fusion robust without per-query score calibration. That rank-based blending is exactly why hybrid is stable across diverse queries.

followups.q: What indexing decisions most affect retrieval quality before any query tuning?
followups.a: Chunk size/overlap, the embedding model and its dimensionality, which fields are searchable vs filterable, and whether text fields carry semantically rich content all set the ceiling on relevance. Bad chunking (too large, split mid-thought) or an embedding mismatch starves even a perfect query. You also need vector + text fields populated for hybrid to work — query tuning can't rescue a poor index.

q: Explain how the semantic ranker in Azure AI Search differs from the initial BM25/vector score.
a: The first-pass retrieval produces a candidate set ranked by BM25 (text), RRF (hybrid), or vector similarity — these are lexical/embedding signals, fast but shallow on meaning. The semantic ranker takes those top candidates and re-scores them with a transformer-based language model that reads the query and passage text together, producing a reranker score and optionally extractive captions and answers. It runs only on the top results (e.g. top 50) for cost, so it sharpens precision at the head of the list rather than improving recall. It needs semantically rich text fields to work — reranking GUIDs or codes gains nothing.
fj: The semantic ranker replaces vector search and improves recall across the whole index.
fs: It reranks only the top candidates with a language model for precision; recall still comes from the first-pass BM25/vector/hybrid retrieval.

followups.q: Concretely, how many candidates does the semantic ranker process, and why is that cap there?
followups.a: It reranks up to the top 50 documents from the first-pass result set — even if more match, only those 50 progress to semantic ranking. The cap exists because running a language-model reranker over the whole result set would be slow and costly, so it's reserved for the head where precision matters most. This is why first-pass recall must put the right docs in that top 50 — the ranker can't recover what didn't make the cut.

followups.q: What's the range of the `@search.rerankerScore` and how would you use it as a confidence gate?
followups.a: The reranker score ranges from 0 to 4 (low to high relevance), independent of the first-pass `@search.score`. You can threshold on it — e.g. drop or flag chunks below ~1.5–2 before feeding context to the LLM — to avoid grounding on weakly relevant passages. Using it as a relevance gate cuts noise in the prompt and reduces hallucination from off-topic context.

followups.q: Beyond a reranker score, what do semantic captions and semantic answers give you?
followups.a: Captions return extractive, highlighted snippets of the most relevant passage text per result, and semantic answers surface a verbatim span that directly answers the query when one exists. These are useful for showing citations/highlights in a UI and for tighter grounding context. They're extractive (pulled from your documents), not generated, so they don't hallucinate — but they only appear when the content supports them.

q: A RAG app on Azure returns confident but wrong answers. Walk through where you'd look first.
a: Start at retrieval, not the model: confirm the right passages are even in the top-k by inspecting search results for sample questions — bad chunking, missing hybrid/semantic ranking, or wrong embedding model starves the prompt. Then check the prompt actually constrains the model to answer only from retrieved context and to say 'I don't know' otherwise, and that citations map to real chunks. Add Content Safety groundedness detection to flag responses unsupported by the sources, and build a labeled eval set so you measure groundedness and retrieval recall instead of guessing. Most 'hallucination' bugs are retrieval or prompting failures, not the model lying.
fj: Confident wrong answers mean the model is broken, so swap to a bigger model.
fs: Debug retrieval first (chunking/hybrid/recall), then the grounding prompt; add groundedness detection + an eval set. Usually it's retrieval, not the model.

followups.q: How do you isolate whether the failure is retrieval or generation?
followups.a: Inspect the retrieved chunks directly for the failing question: if the correct passage isn't in the top-k, it's a retrieval/recall problem (chunking, embeddings, hybrid/semantic config); if the right passage was retrieved but the answer still contradicts it, it's a generation/grounding-prompt problem. Splitting the pipeline this way turns a vague 'hallucination' into a measurable, fixable stage rather than guessing.

followups.q: What concrete metrics would you put on the labeled eval set to catch this in CI?
followups.a: Track retrieval metrics like recall@k / hit-rate (was the gold passage retrieved) and generation metrics like groundedness/faithfulness (is each claim supported by context) and answer relevance. Run them per release so a model upgrade or chunking change that regresses surfaces as a number, not a user complaint. Groundedness detection can auto-label ungrounded segments to feed this.

followups.q: When is swapping to a bigger model actually the right fix here?
followups.a: Only after retrieval is verified correct and the prompt properly constrains grounding — if the right context is present and well-prompted but the model still mis-synthesizes or ignores instructions, a stronger model (or fine-tuned format adherence) can help. But that's the last lever, not the first; most confident-wrong cases are starved retrieval, and a bigger model just hallucinates more expensively on bad context.

q: What is Microsoft Copilot Studio, and what are the core building blocks of a custom agent in it?
a: Copilot Studio is the low-code tool for building custom copilots/agents and publishing them to channels like Teams, web, and Microsoft 365. The classic building blocks are topics (authored conversation paths triggered by phrases), actions/tools (calls to Power Automate flows, connectors, APIs, or other agents), and knowledge sources (websites, SharePoint, Dataverse, files) the agent can ground answers on. Modern Copilot Studio adds generative orchestration, where an LLM planner picks which topics, tools, knowledge, and child agents to invoke instead of relying purely on hand-built routing. You wire data and logic with little code and govern it through Power Platform.
fj: Copilot Studio requires you to write the orchestration code that routes between tools yourself.
fs: Low-code agent builder: topics + actions/tools + knowledge, with an LLM-driven generative orchestrator choosing among them.

followups.q: How do you connect a Copilot Studio agent to a system it doesn't natively support?
followups.a: Add an action backed by a Power Automate flow, a prebuilt/custom connector, or a REST API call (including via the connector framework), so the agent can read/write to that system as a tool. For complex logic you wrap it in a flow; for standard SaaS you use an existing connector. This is how a low-code agent reaches arbitrary enterprise systems without hand-written orchestration code.

followups.q: What governance does building on Power Platform actually buy you?
followups.a: Agents live in Power Platform environments with Dataverse, so you get environment-based isolation (dev/test/prod), Data Loss Prevention policies that restrict which connectors agents may combine, Entra-based access control, and admin-center monitoring. That lets an admin govern citizen-developer agents centrally. The trade is you operate within Power Platform's model rather than raw Azure resources.

followups.q: When would knowledge sources alone not be enough and you'd need an action instead?
followups.a: Knowledge sources ground answers on relatively static content (SharePoint, websites, files, Dataverse) for read-style Q&A. When the agent must take an action or fetch live, parameterized data — create a ticket, look up a specific order status, post to a system — you need an action/tool, because knowledge retrieval can't perform writes or call APIs with runtime inputs. Q&A uses knowledge; transactions use actions.

q: In Copilot Studio, what is generative orchestration and how does it change topic-based authoring?
a: Classic authoring relied on trigger-phrase topics with deterministic routing — you hand-built the conversation tree. Generative orchestration inserts an LLM planning layer that reads user intent, decomposes multi-step requests, and dynamically selects among topics, tools/actions, knowledge sources, and child agents, then executes a plan with safety guardrails. Topics become reusable building blocks that return output variables to the orchestrator rather than always sending a final message, so the planner can compose them. This trades some determinism for flexibility, so you steer it with high-quality instructions and tool descriptions instead of exhaustive trigger phrases.
fj: Generative orchestration just auto-generates more trigger phrases for your existing topics.
fs: An LLM planner dynamically composes topics/tools/knowledge/child-agents per request, instead of fixed trigger-phrase routing.

followups.q: If routing is now LLM-driven, how do you make tools and topics reliably selectable?
followups.a: You write clear, descriptive names and descriptions for each topic/action plus strong top-level agent instructions, because the planner picks based on those descriptions much like LLM tool-calling. Vague or overlapping descriptions cause mis-selection. So the authoring effort shifts from enumerating trigger phrases to crafting precise tool/topic descriptions and instructions the planner can reason over.

followups.q: How do you keep a critical, must-be-deterministic flow from being skipped by the planner?
followups.a: For flows that must run exactly (compliance disclosures, auth, structured intake), you still use a deterministically triggered topic or constrain the path so the orchestrator can't reroute around it. You blend deterministic topics for high-stakes steps with generative orchestration for open-ended navigation. The lesson: generative orchestration is for flexibility, not for steps where you can't tolerate the planner improvising.

followups.q: What new failure modes does generative orchestration introduce that classic routing didn't?
followups.a: Non-determinism: the same prompt can take different paths, pick the wrong tool, loop, or hallucinate a plan, and behavior can drift as the underlying model updates. That makes testing and reproducibility harder than fixed trigger trees. You mitigate with eval suites, observability/transcripts, guardrails, and constraining critical paths — accepting that you trade predictability for flexibility.

q: When should a team build a copilot in Copilot Studio versus a custom engine agent in Azure AI Foundry?
a: Copilot Studio is the right call for low-code, fast time-to-value agents aimed at business users — Teams/M365 deployment, Power Platform connectors and governance, and natural-language authoring without managing models or compute. Choose Foundry's Agent Service when you need full code control: a specific model from the catalog, custom orchestration (Agent Framework/LangGraph), bespoke tool logic, fine-grained networking and identity, or tight integration with Azure services. Microsoft 365 Copilot's Agent Builder sits even further toward no-code for single-team agents. The driver is control and customization versus speed and citizen-developer accessibility.
fj: Copilot Studio and Foundry agents are interchangeable, so pick whichever you know.
fs: Copilot Studio = low-code, M365/Power Platform, business users; Foundry agents = full code/model/network control for developers.

followups.q: Can the two coexist — e.g. a Copilot Studio front-end calling a Foundry agent?
followups.a: Yes — a common pattern is a Copilot Studio agent in Teams/M365 that invokes a code-built Foundry Agent Service agent as a tool/child agent for the heavy custom logic. You get Copilot Studio's channel reach and citizen-developer surface plus Foundry's model and orchestration control behind it. They're complementary layers, not strictly either/or.

followups.q: What specifically can't Copilot Studio do that forces you down to Foundry's Agent Service?
followups.a: Picking a specific catalog model or fine-tune, writing custom multi-agent orchestration in code (Agent Framework/LangGraph), bespoke tool logic, strict network isolation via private endpoints/managed VNet, and bring-your-own data stores in your tenant. Copilot Studio abstracts the model and compute away, so deep control over those forces the code-first Agent Service. The dividing line is control over model, orchestration, and infrastructure.

followups.q: Where does Microsoft 365 Copilot Agent Builder fit relative to these two?
followups.a: Agent Builder (declarative agents in M365 Copilot) is the most no-code, single-team option — quick agents grounded on M365 content, built inside Copilot, with the least customization. Copilot Studio is the middle low-code tier with connectors and broader channels; Foundry Agent Service is the full code-first tier. You move down the ladder as you need more control and away from it as you prioritize speed and accessibility.

q: What was prompt flow in Azure AI Foundry, and what is its current status?
a: Prompt flow was the visual orchestration tool for LLM apps — a DAG of LLM, prompt, and Python tool nodes you could prototype, run in batch, and evaluate, including dedicated evaluation flows that scored a run against criteria like groundedness or relevance. It supported variants for A/B-style prompt comparison and one-click deployment. Importantly, Microsoft ended prompt flow feature development on April 20, 2026 and plans full retirement on April 20, 2027, steering new orchestration work to the Microsoft Agent Framework. So you should know the concepts for interviews but build new pipelines on the Agent Framework.
fj: Prompt flow is the recommended way to build new LLM orchestration on Azure today.
fs: Prompt flow = visual LLM DAG + eval flows, but it's being retired (dev ended Apr 2026, retires Apr 2027) in favor of the Agent Framework.

followups.q: What is the Microsoft Agent Framework and what lineage does it come from?
followups.a: The Microsoft Agent Framework is the open-source, production-oriented SDK/runtime for building agents and agentic workflows, GA as of April 2026. It unifies the lineage of Semantic Kernel (enterprise integration, plugins) and AutoGen (multi-agent orchestration) into one supported framework. It's Microsoft's recommended target for new orchestration work that prompt flow used to cover.

followups.q: What happens to existing prompt flows between now and the 2027 retirement?
followups.a: During deprecation existing flows keep running and receive security and critical bug fixes only — no new features, and container images stop getting updates. At full retirement (April 20, 2027) the web authoring experience, VS Code extensions, and container images are no longer supported or available. So existing flows operate for now, but you should plan migration well before that date.

followups.q: Prompt flow's eval flows scored groundedness/relevance — where does that capability live now?
followups.a: Evaluation moved into Foundry's built-in evaluation tooling (the evaluators SDK and portal evaluations) plus Agent Framework integration, which score groundedness, relevance, coherence, safety, and custom metrics over datasets. You no longer need a dedicated prompt-flow eval DAG; you call the evaluation library against your test set. The eval concept survives — the implementation moved off prompt flow.

q: What is the Azure AI Foundry Agent Service, and how does it differ from the older Assistants API?
a: Foundry Agent Service is the managed platform for building, deploying, and scaling AI agents with built-in tools (file search, code interpreter, web search, MCP tool calling) and enterprise controls for identity, networking, and safety. It replaces the Azure OpenAI Assistants API, which is deprecated and retires August 26, 2026. Architecturally the new service is built on the Responses API and preserves the same threads/runs object model as the Assistants API (threads, messages, and runs remain the primary REST primitives); 'conversations' is portal/narrative language, not a renamed API object, and it supports both portal-authored prompt agents and code-hosted agents written with frameworks like Agent Framework or LangGraph. Migrating off Assistants before the retirement date is the action item.
fj: The Assistants API is the current, recommended way to build agents on Azure.
fs: Assistants API is deprecated (retires Aug 2026); use Foundry Agent Service — built on the Responses API, 'conversations' not 'threads,' with managed tools.

followups.q: Concretely, how do Assistants-API concepts map onto the Responses/Agent-Service model?
followups.a: The Assistant becomes a defined Agent, The correct migration mapping is: assistants → agents (same concept, new service endpoint), threads → threads, runs → runs. The Responses API is the new inference backbone but the Agent Service surface still exposes threads/runs.. You define one Agent with its instructions/tools/model, then per session create a conversation and send messages against the published agent endpoint. So the migration is mostly a concept remap plus pointing at the Responses-based endpoint, not a from-scratch rewrite.

followups.q: What does built-in MCP tool calling let an agent do that custom function tools didn't?
followups.a: MCP (Model Context Protocol) support lets the agent connect to external MCP servers to discover and call their tools dynamically over a standard protocol, instead of you hand-registering each function. This makes tools portable and reusable across agents/runtimes and lets the agent reach third-party MCP tool servers. It standardizes tool integration rather than locking tools to one SDK's function-calling shape.

followups.q: If a team is on Assistants today, what's the concrete risk of not migrating by August 26, 2026?
followups.a: After the retirement date the Assistants API stops being available, so any workload still calling it breaks — not degrades, breaks. The mitigation is migrating to Foundry Agent Service (Responses-based) ahead of the date, remapping threads→conversations and assistants→agents and re-testing tools. Treat the date as a hard deadline, not a soft deprecation warning.

q: Walk through grounding a Foundry agent with your own data using the file search / AI Search tool.
a: You attach a knowledge tool to the agent — either built-in file search over uploaded documents, or a connection to an Azure AI Search index for production-scale RAG. At runtime the agent decides when to call the tool, retrieves relevant chunks, and grounds its reply with citations back to the source. For real systems you point it at AI Search so you control chunking, hybrid + semantic retrieval, and access scope, rather than the simpler built-in vector store. In standard enterprise setups the agent's data (threads/conversations, files, vector store) lands in your bring-your-own Storage, AI Search, and Cosmos DB so it stays in your tenant and region.
fj: The agent automatically knows your company data once you create it.
fs: Attach a file-search or AI Search tool; the agent retrieves and cites chunks at runtime. Use AI Search for prod RAG; data lands in your BYO stores.

code.label: create an agent with a tool

followups.q: Why prefer the AI Search tool over built-in file search for a production agent?
followups.a: Built-in file search uses a managed vector store that's quick to start but gives you little control. The AI Search tool lets you own chunking, hybrid + semantic retrieval, filters, security trimming, and scaling — the levers that drive relevance and access control at scale. For prototypes file search is fine; for production RAG you want the AI Search index you can tune and govern.

followups.q: How does per-user access control on retrieved documents work when the agent searches your index?
followups.a: You enforce document-level security by applying filters at query time (e.g. security-trimming on a group/ACL field) so the agent only retrieves chunks the calling user is entitled to, often by passing the user's group claims into the search filter. The agent itself doesn't grant access — your index design and query filters do. Skipping this leaks restricted content into answers, so it's a core part of grounding setup.

followups.q: In a standard agent setup, where does conversation and file data actually live?
followups.a: In the standard (bring-your-own) setup, the agent's state — conversations/threads, uploaded files, and the vector store — is persisted in your own Azure Storage, Azure AI Search, and Cosmos DB resources, inside your tenant and chosen region. That's how you keep data residency and control versus a fully Microsoft-hosted store. It matters for compliance: your data doesn't leave resources you own.

q: What does Azure Machine Learning give you that Azure AI Foundry's GenAI tooling does not?
a: Azure ML is the full classic-ML and MLOps platform: workspaces, compute clusters/instances, datasets, training jobs and pipelines, AutoML for automated model selection, a model registry, and MLflow-based experiment tracking. It's where you train and manage your own models — regression, classification, deep learning — not just consume foundation models. Foundry focuses on GenAI app development (catalog, agents, RAG, eval), and actually builds its managed-compute and some pipeline capabilities on Azure ML underneath. So you use Azure ML for custom model training and lifecycle, and Foundry for orchestrating LLM-powered applications.
fj: Azure ML is just an older version of Azure AI Foundry for the same GenAI tasks.
fs: Azure ML = train/manage your own models + MLOps (compute, AutoML, MLflow, registry); Foundry = build GenAI apps on foundation models.

followups.q: Give a concrete task where you'd reach for Azure ML, not Foundry's GenAI tooling.
followups.a: Training a custom gradient-boosted or deep-learning model on your tabular/image data — say a churn or fraud classifier — with AutoML for model selection, hyperparameter sweeps on a compute cluster, MLflow tracking, and a registered model deployed to an online endpoint. That's bespoke supervised learning on your data, which Foundry's foundation-model app tooling doesn't address. Foundry consumes models; Azure ML builds them.

followups.q: If Foundry builds managed compute on Azure ML underneath, what does that imply operationally?
followups.a: It means Foundry's managed-compute model deployments and some pipelines inherit Azure ML constructs (compute, endpoints, quotas), so concepts like online/batch endpoints and compute sizing carry over, and Azure ML quota can affect Foundry compute. Knowing the shared substrate helps you debug capacity and networking issues that surface in Foundry but originate in the Azure ML layer.

followups.q: Can you fine-tune or host open foundation models in Azure ML rather than Foundry?
followups.a: Yes — Azure ML supports fine-tuning and deploying open models (e.g. `Llama`, `Phi`) to managed compute endpoints, and the model catalog's managed-compute deployments run on Azure ML infrastructure. So foundation models aren't exclusively a Foundry concern; Azure ML handles the training/compute side. The split is app-orchestration (Foundry) vs model training and dedicated hosting (Azure ML).

q: How does MLflow integrate with Azure Machine Learning for tracking and deployment?
a: Azure ML is MLflow-compatible: you point the MLflow tracking URI at your workspace and your existing mlflow.log_metric/log_model calls write runs, metrics, params, and artifacts into Azure ML, which is the recommended tracking approach. Logging a model in MLflow format captures its signature and environment, so deploying it to a managed online endpoint is no-code — Azure ML auto-generates the scoring script and environment, no hand-written inference code needed. AutoML models work the same no-code way. Note Microsoft is retiring MLproject-file support in September 2026, but MLflow tracking and model format remain fully supported.
fj: Using MLflow on Azure requires a totally different SDK than open-source MLflow.
fs: Set the tracking URI to the workspace and standard MLflow calls just work; MLflow-format models deploy no-code to managed online endpoints.

code.label: point MLflow at the workspace

followups.q: What does the MLflow model 'signature' actually buy you at deployment time?
followups.a: The signature records the expected input/output schema (column names, types, tensor shapes), so Azure ML can auto-generate a validated scoring script and reject malformed requests at the endpoint. It's what makes no-code deployment possible and catches schema drift between training and serving. Without a signature you'd hand-write inference and lose that input validation.

followups.q: If MLproject-file support is retiring in September 2026, what breaks and what doesn't?
followups.a: Workflows that rely on `MLproject` files to package and run reproducible MLflow projects on Azure ML lose that path and must move to Azure ML jobs/pipelines for orchestration. MLflow tracking (logging runs/metrics/models) and the MLflow model format for no-code deployment remain fully supported. So retrain/orchestrate via Azure ML jobs, but keep using MLflow logging and model packaging.

followups.q: How do you make MLflow logging capture metrics automatically without instrumenting every call?
followups.a: Call `mlflow.autolog()` (or framework-specific autolog) after setting the tracking URI, and supported libraries — scikit-learn, XGBoost, PyTorch, etc. — auto-log params, metrics, and the model on `fit`. It cuts boilerplate and ensures consistent run records into Azure ML. You can still add explicit `log_metric` calls for custom metrics autolog doesn't capture.

q: Compare a managed online endpoint and a batch endpoint in Azure Machine Learning.
a: A managed online endpoint serves real-time, synchronous, low-latency inference over HTTPS, with Azure managing the underlying compute, autoscaling, and safe blue/green rollout via traffic splitting across deployments. A batch endpoint scores large volumes asynchronously — you submit a dataset, it runs on a compute cluster and writes results to storage, ideal for nightly scoring where latency doesn't matter and per-request cost would be wasteful. Online endpoints keep compute warm (you pay for it idle); batch endpoints spin compute up per job. Choose by latency need: interactive request/response versus bulk offline scoring.
fj: Online and batch endpoints are the same thing with different names.
fs: Online = real-time HTTPS, warm compute, blue/green traffic split; batch = async bulk scoring on a cluster that scales per job.

followups.q: How does autoscaling differ in spirit between the two endpoint types?
followups.a: Online endpoints autoscale instance count against live traffic/metrics (CPU, RPS) to keep latency low, but they keep a warm minimum you pay for even when idle. Batch endpoints don't 'autoscale to traffic' — they parallelize a submitted job across a compute cluster's nodes and the cluster can scale to zero between jobs. One optimizes steady low-latency serving; the other optimizes throughput per batch run.

followups.q: What's the cost trap of an online endpoint serving a low-traffic model?
followups.a: It bills for warm compute continuously to stay ready, so a rarely-hit model wastes money sitting idle. For sporadic or bulk workloads a batch endpoint (compute spins up per job, scales to zero after) is far cheaper. The senior move is matching endpoint type to traffic shape — don't keep GPUs warm for a model that's called twice a day.

followups.q: Can you do safe rollout (canary) on a batch endpoint the way you can online?
followups.a: Batch endpoints support multiple deployments with a default deployment, but they don't do live percentage traffic splitting like online endpoints — invocations route to the named/default deployment per job rather than splitting a stream. Canary/blue-green traffic ramps are an online-endpoint capability. For batch you validate a new deployment on sample jobs, then switch the default.

q: How would you do a safe, gradual rollout of a new model version on an Azure ML managed online endpoint?
a: Managed online endpoints separate the endpoint (stable URL/auth) from deployments (a model+env+compute). You create a second deployment with the new model alongside the live one, then shift traffic gradually — e.g. 90/10, watch latency, errors, and quality, then ramp to 100% — using the endpoint's traffic-split percentages. If metrics regress you flip traffic back to the old deployment instantly, since it's still running. This is blue/green / canary without DNS changes, and you only delete the old deployment once the new one is proven. The endpoint URL never changes for callers.
fj: You overwrite the existing deployment in place and hope the new model is fine.
fs: Add a second deployment, split traffic (canary 90/10 → ramp), monitor, and roll back by reverting the split — endpoint URL stays constant.

code.label: canary traffic split

followups.q: How do you direct test traffic to the new deployment without affecting production users?
followups.a: Use the mirror/test traffic capability or the deployment-specific invocation header (`azureml-model-deployment`) to send synthetic or shadow requests to `green` while live users still hit `blue`. That lets you validate the new model on real-shaped inputs before assigning it any production percentage. So you warm and validate the deployment first, then start the percentage ramp.

followups.q: What signals decide whether you ramp up or roll back during the canary?
followups.a: Operational signals — latency (p95/p99), error rate, and saturation on the new deployment — plus quality signals from logged predictions or an eval set if you can score them. If any regress beyond threshold you revert traffic to `blue` instantly. The point of keeping the old deployment running is zero-downtime rollback, so you ramp only when both ops and quality hold.

followups.q: Why keep the endpoint and deployment as separate objects at all?
followups.a: Separation gives callers a stable URL and auth while you swap models behind it — multiple deployments can coexist under one endpoint, traffic splits among them, and rollback is a config change, not a redeploy or DNS edit. If endpoint and model were one object every model change would churn the caller-facing contract. The indirection is what makes blue/green possible.

q: What is Azure AI Document Intelligence, and how do prebuilt, layout, and custom models differ?
a: Document Intelligence (formerly Form Recognizer) extracts structured data — text, key-value pairs, tables, selection marks — from documents via OCR plus ML. Prebuilt models target common document types out of the box (invoices, receipts, IDs, business cards, W-2s) with no training. The layout model returns structure — text, tables, selection marks, headings, and reading order — useful as the chunking front-end for RAG over PDFs. Custom models you train on your own labeled forms: template models for fixed layouts and neural models for varied/mixed layouts, and you can compose several into one. Use prebuilt if a type fits, custom when your documents are bespoke.
fj: You must train a custom model for every document, even standard invoices.
fs: Prebuilt for common types (invoices/receipts/IDs), layout for structure/tables/reading-order, custom (template vs neural) for your own forms.

followups.q: When would you choose a custom neural model over a custom template model?
followups.a: Template (formerly 'custom form') models learn fixed-position layouts and train fast on a handful of consistent samples — great when every document looks the same. Neural models handle structural variation and mixed/unstructured layouts where fields move around, at the cost of more training data/time. So: rigid, uniform forms → template; varied or semi-structured documents → neural.

followups.q: Why is the layout model a natural front-end for RAG over PDFs?
followups.a: Layout returns reading order, tables, headings, and section structure (including a Markdown-style output), so you can chunk along real semantic boundaries instead of blindly splitting raw OCR text. That preserves table integrity and logical sections, which improves retrieval relevance downstream. Feeding clean, structure-aware chunks into AI Search beats naive page-splitting.

followups.q: What does composing multiple custom models into one buy you?
followups.a: A composed model lets a single endpoint classify the incoming document and route it to the right sub-model automatically, so you handle several form types (say invoices from different vendors) through one call without pre-sorting inputs. It trades a bit of classification overhead for operational simplicity. You build/label each type, then compose so the service picks the matching model at inference.

q: Compare the Azure AI Vision, Speech, and Language services and when each fits.
a: These are the task-specific 'Foundry Tools' (formerly Cognitive Services) for non-generative AI. Vision handles images — OCR/Read, image analysis/tagging, and Face. Speech handles audio — speech-to-text, text-to-speech, translation, and speaker recognition. Language handles text — sentiment, key phrases, NER and PII detection, summarization, Conversational Language Understanding (CLU) for intent, and custom question answering. You pick these over an LLM when you want a deterministic, cheaper, lower-latency, pretrained API for a narrow task — e.g. transcribing calls or redacting PII — rather than paying for a general model. They're increasingly accessed and authored inside the Foundry portal.
fj: You should always use a GPT model instead of these narrow services.
fs: Vision (images), Speech (audio), Language (text NLP) = cheap, fast, pretrained task APIs; use them over an LLM for narrow deterministic tasks.

followups.q: Give a pipeline where these services and an LLM are used together, not as alternatives.
followups.a: Call-center analytics: Speech-to-Text transcribes the call, the Language service's PII detection redacts sensitive spans, and an LLM then summarizes the cleaned transcript and drafts a follow-up. The narrow services do deterministic, cheap heavy-lifting (transcription, redaction); the LLM does open-ended synthesis. Composing them is cheaper and safer than asking one general model to do everything.

followups.q: Why might PII redaction via the Language service be preferable to asking an LLM to redact?
followups.a: The Language PII detection is a purpose-built, deterministic classifier with defined entity categories and confidence scores, so it's cheaper, lower-latency, auditable, and more consistent than a generative model that may miss or paraphrase. For a compliance control you want repeatable, testable behavior, not the variability of an LLM. Redaction is exactly the deterministic-task case these services exist for.

followups.q: How do custom flavors like CLU or Custom Vision differ from the prebuilt models?
followups.a: Prebuilt models work out of the box on general tasks; custom flavors — Conversational Language Understanding (intents/entities), Custom Text Classification/NER, Custom Vision, Custom Speech — you train on your own labeled data to fit domain-specific vocabulary or classes. You use prebuilt when general accuracy suffices and go custom when your domain terms or categories aren't covered. It's the same train-when-bespoke pattern as Document Intelligence.

q: When and how would you fine-tune a model in Azure OpenAI, and what methods are available?
a: Fine-tune when prompting and RAG plateau and you need consistent format/style/tone or to compress long instructions into the weights — not to teach the model new facts (that's RAG's job). Azure OpenAI supports supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on labeled input/output pairs and direct preference optimization (DPO) to align toward preferred-over-rejected responses; you can stack them (SFT then DPO). Models like GPT-4.1 and 4o families are supported, and you can deploy the resulting custom model as Standard, Global Standard, or provisioned. Note global-standard fine-tuned deployments may store weights outside your region, which matters for data residency.
fj: Fine-tuning is how you give the model your company's current knowledge.
fs: Fine-tune for format/style/compression, not facts (use RAG for facts); SFT for I/O pairs, DPO for preferences, stackable, then deploy the custom model.

followups.q: Why is fine-tuning the wrong tool for keeping a model current on changing facts?
followups.a: Weights are frozen at training time, so a fine-tuned model goes stale the moment facts change and you'd have to re-train to update — slow and costly. RAG injects fresh facts at query time from an index you can update continuously, with citations. Fine-tuning teaches behavior (format/style/tone); RAG supplies knowledge. Using fine-tuning for facts bakes in staleness and offers no provenance.

followups.q: When does DPO add value over plain SFT, and why stack them?
followups.a: SFT teaches the model to imitate good outputs, but it can't easily express 'prefer A over B' on subtle quality dimensions. DPO optimizes directly on preference pairs (chosen vs rejected), aligning tone, helpfulness, or safety preferences. Stacking — SFT first to establish capability, then DPO to refine preferences — gives both correct behavior and aligned style. DPO alone without a competent base is weaker.

followups.q: What deployment/residency caveat comes with a fine-tuned model on Global Standard?
followups.a: Global Standard routes/processes traffic across regions and can store the fine-tuned weights outside your chosen region, so for strict data residency you'd pick a Regional or Data Zone deployment instead. The trade is availability/throughput vs locality. Always check where the custom weights and inference data reside before choosing Global for a regulated workload.

q: In the Azure AI model catalog, when do you choose serverless API (MaaS) versus managed compute deployment?
a: Serverless API / Models-as-a-Service hosts the model on Microsoft-managed infrastructure behind a pay-per-token (or provisioned) API — no GPU to manage, fast to provision, with global/data-zone/regional processing variants for compliance; ideal for variable load and quick starts. Managed compute deploys the model onto dedicated Azure ML VMs in your subscription that you size and pay for by the hour whether or not it's used — needed for models not offered as MaaS, for full network isolation, or for sustained high throughput where dedicated GPUs are cheaper. Decide by control and utilization: serverless for convenience and spiky traffic, managed compute for isolation, exotic models, or steady heavy load.
fj: Managed compute is always cheaper because there's no per-token charge.
fs: Serverless/MaaS = managed, per-token, spiky/quick; managed compute = your dedicated VMs billed hourly, for isolation, non-MaaS models, or steady load.

followups.q: Why is 'no per-token charge' on managed compute not the same as cheaper?
followups.a: Managed compute bills the GPU VM by the hour whether or not it's serving requests, so a lightly used model pays for idle capacity. Serverless only charges per token consumed. Managed compute wins only when utilization is high and sustained enough that the hourly GPU cost beats the equivalent token spend. At low/spiky traffic, per-token is usually cheaper despite 'no token charge.'

followups.q: What would force you onto managed compute even if serverless were cheaper?
followups.a: A model not offered as a serverless/MaaS endpoint, a requirement for full network isolation (the model VM inside your VNet with private endpoints, no shared multitenant inference), strict residency, or needing custom runtime/configuration. Those are control and compliance drivers that serverless can't meet. Cost is only one axis — isolation and model availability can override it.

followups.q: How do the global, data-zone, and regional processing variants of serverless differ?
followups.a: They control where inference is processed: Regional keeps it in one Azure region, Data Zone confines it to a geography (e.g. US or EU data zone), and Global can route across regions for the best availability/capacity. You pick the most permissive one your compliance allows — Global for max throughput/availability, Data Zone/Regional for residency. Same model, different processing-locality guarantees.

q: How does Azure AI Content Safety help, and what does groundedness detection add for RAG?
a: Content Safety is a standalone service that classifies text and images across hate, sexual, violence, and self-harm at severity levels, plus Prompt Shields against (indirect) prompt injection, protected-material detection for copyrighted text/code, and custom categories. For RAG specifically, groundedness detection checks whether an LLM response is actually supported by the provided source passages and flags ungrounded segments; in reasoning mode it explains why, and a correction feature can rewrite ungrounded text against the sources. This is your automated check that the model answered from retrieved context rather than inventing — note these models are English-only today.
fj: Content filtering and groundedness detection are the same control.
fs: Content Safety = harm categories + Prompt Shields + protected material; groundedness detection separately flags (and can correct) ungrounded RAG answers.

followups.q: What attack do Prompt Shields specifically defend against, including the indirect variant?
followups.a: Prompt Shields detect jailbreak/prompt-injection attempts in user input and, importantly, indirect (document) injection where malicious instructions are embedded in retrieved content or files the model ingests. The indirect case is the RAG-specific risk: a poisoned source document trying to hijack the model. Shields flag those so you can block or strip them before they steer the model.

followups.q: How does groundedness detection's correction feature work, and what's the catch?
followups.a: When a response contains ungrounded claims, the correction (reasoning) mode can rewrite the offending segments to align with the provided sources, optionally explaining why they were ungrounded. The catch: it requires you to supply the grounding sources and the models are English-only today, so it's not a universal safety net. It's a corrective layer on top of, not a replacement for, good retrieval and prompting.

followups.q: Is Content Safety only usable through Azure OpenAI's built-in content filter, or standalone?
followups.a: It's a standalone service with its own APIs (text/image moderation, Prompt Shields, groundedness, protected material), so you can call it on any content — your own models, non-AOAI models, or pre/post-processing around a pipeline — not just inside the AOAI filter. The AOAI built-in filter uses the same family, but standalone gives you placement control. That lets you apply safety where you choose in the flow.

q: How do you secure an Azure AI Foundry / OpenAI workload for an enterprise: networking, identity, and data residency?
a: Lock down ingress by disabling public network access and exposing the Foundry/OpenAI resource only through Private Endpoints (Private Link), and enable a managed virtual network so agent outbound traffic to Storage, AI Search, and Cosmos DB also flows over private endpoints. Use Microsoft Entra managed identity instead of API keys so the resource authenticates to dependencies with RBAC and no secrets to leak. For data residency, pick regional/data-zone deployment processing and bring-your-own Storage/Search/Cosmos so agent and conversation data rests in your tenant and chosen geography — and remember global deployments can process or store data outside your region. Layer in content safety and audit logging.
fj: API keys plus the default public endpoint are sufficient enterprise security.
fs: Private Link + managed VNet + Entra managed identity (no keys) + regional/data-zone deployments with BYO stores for residency, plus content safety/audit.

followups.q: Why is a managed VNet needed in addition to a private endpoint on the resource itself?
followups.a: A private endpoint on the Foundry/AOAI resource secures inbound traffic, but an agent also makes outbound calls to Storage, AI Search, and Cosmos DB. The managed VNet routes that egress over private endpoints too, so data never traverses the public internet in either direction. Securing only ingress leaves the agent's backend dependencies exposed — you need both halves for true isolation.

followups.q: Concretely, what does using managed identity over API keys prevent?
followups.a: Keys are long-lived shared secrets that leak via config, logs, or source control and grant broad access with no per-caller attribution. Entra managed identity issues short-lived tokens, scopes access via RBAC roles, supports rotation and conditional access, and ties actions to an identity for audit. So you eliminate a stealable static credential and get least-privilege, auditable access instead.

followups.q: A workload must keep all data in the EU — which deployment and storage choices enforce that?
followups.a: Choose Data Zone (EU) or a specific EU Regional deployment so inference is processed only within the EU data zone, and bring your own Storage/AI Search/Cosmos provisioned in EU regions for agent/conversation data. Avoid Global deployments, which can process or store outside the region. Pairing data-zone processing with EU-region BYO stores is what actually enforces residency.

q: Outline the main cost drivers of a GenAI workload on Azure and how you'd control them.
a: The big levers are model inference (per-token standard vs reserved PTUs/provisioned — PTUs win only at sustained high utilization), retrieval infra (Azure AI Search tier and replica/partition units, semantic-ranker query volume), and any managed compute or always-on online endpoints billed hourly whether idle. Control them by right-sizing models (use smaller/mini models where quality allows), caching and trimming prompts and retrieved context to cut tokens, scaling search to load instead of over-provisioning, and shutting down or autoscaling idle compute and endpoints. Attribute spend with Azure Cost Management and set budgets/alerts so regressions surface fast. Match reserved capacity to genuinely steady demand only.
fj: The model token bill is the only cost that matters in a GenAI app.
fs: Inference tokens (standard vs PTU), AI Search units + semantic-ranker calls, and idle managed compute/endpoints; right-size models, trim tokens, autoscale, and set budgets.

followups.q: At what point do provisioned throughput units (PTUs) actually beat pay-per-token?
followups.a: PTUs reserve dedicated capacity at a fixed cost, so they pay off only when utilization is high and sustained — enough steady token volume and latency-sensitivity that you'd otherwise hit throttling on standard. For spiky or low-volume traffic the reserved capacity sits underused and pay-per-token is cheaper. Size PTUs to genuinely steady demand; mixing PTU baseline with standard spillover is a common hybrid.

followups.q: Which prompt-engineering levers most directly cut the token bill?
followups.a: Trim retrieved context to only the top-k truly relevant chunks (not everything that matched), cap output with `max_tokens`, prune verbose system prompts, and cache or reuse stable prefixes where supported. Since you pay per input + output token, smaller well-targeted context and bounded responses cut spend directly. Over-stuffing context is both costlier and often less accurate — trimming helps twice.

followups.q: What hidden Azure AI Search costs surprise teams that only budgeted for the LLM?
followups.a: Search bills on the service tier and the number of replica/partition search units you provision (running continuously, not per query), plus semantic-ranker usage which is metered by query volume on a separate plan. Over-provisioning units or running semantic ranking on every query inflates cost independent of token spend. Scale units to load and apply semantic ranking selectively to control it.

q: Azure Machine Learning pipelines run training jobs across compute clusters. What is the correct SDK v2 pattern to define and submit a multi-step training pipeline, and how does the component-based approach differ from the legacy estimator pattern?
a: In Azure ML SDK v2, pipelines are composed of reusable `@component`-decorated Python functions or YAML-defined components, each with explicit input/output contracts typed via `Input`/`Output` annotations. You wire components together inside a `@pipeline`-decorated factory function, then submit the resulting `PipelineJob` via `ml_client.jobs.create_or_update()`. The legacy SDK v1 estimator pattern bundled environment, compute, and script into a monolithic `EstimatorStep`, making individual steps hard to cache or reuse independently. In v2, Azure ML automatically caches component outputs keyed on inputs + code hash, so unchanged upstream steps are skipped on resubmission—a critical cost and latency saving in iterative experimentation. Component artifacts flow through named ports, and the orchestrator handles data movement between compute targets. For MLOps maturity, components can be registered in the component registry and versioned, enabling cross-team reuse without copying code.
fj: Describe what a pipeline is and how you run a training script on Azure ML.
fs: Walk through how component output caching works in SDK v2 and when it can cause stale results.

code.label: SDK v2 pipeline with two components
lines: from azure.ai.ml import MLClient, Input, Output
# runs as script inside managed environment

followups.q: How do you pass secrets (like a database password) into a pipeline component without storing them in YAML?
followups.a: Reference an Azure Key Vault secret via an `AzureKeyVaultSecret` environment variable binding in the component environment, or inject it as a `JobService`-level secret URI. The compute identity (managed identity) must have Key Vault `get` permission so the secret is fetched at runtime and never stored in the pipeline graph or logs.

followups.q: What does 'data asset versioning' in Azure ML give you over raw blob paths?
followups.a: Data assets are first-class lineage objects: every pipeline run records which version of a data asset it consumed, enabling full reproducibility audits. You can promote a dataset to 'latest', roll back to a prior version by number, and set access permissions at the asset level rather than the storage account level.

followups.q: How would you configure a pipeline to run on a spot/low-priority cluster and handle preemption?
followups.a: Set `tier='LowPriority'` on the `AmlCompute` provisioning configuration. Azure ML will automatically retry preempted steps up to the configured `max_retry_count`; since v2 component outputs are stored on Azure Storage before the step is marked complete, only the in-flight step needs to replay, not the full pipeline.

q: Azure OpenAI Service exposes the same REST API as OpenAI but with enterprise controls. What are the key differences a senior engineer must account for when migrating an OpenAI-SDK application to Azure OpenAI, and what does 'Provisioned Throughput Units' (PTUs) mean for capacity planning?
a: Azure OpenAI uses a per-deployment endpoint (`https://<resource>.openai.azure.com/openai/deployments/<deployment-name>/chat/completions`) instead of the central api.openai.com endpoint, so all SDK calls must supply `azure_endpoint`, `api_key` (or credential), and `api_version`. The model name in the payload is replaced by the deployment name you created, which can differ from the model ID. Responsible AI content filters are enforced server-side and can raise `content_filter` errors that the application must handle gracefully. PTUs are pre-purchased units that reserve dedicated model capacity; each PTU supports a deterministic number of tokens-per-minute for a specific model, so capacity planning requires projecting peak prompt+completion token throughput and provisioning PTUs to avoid 429s without over-buying. Standard (pay-per-call) deployments share capacity and can be throttled; PTU deployments are isolated but require commitment. Quota is per-region per-subscription, so architects spread high-volume workloads across multiple regions.
fj: Explain what Azure OpenAI Service is and how it differs from calling openai.com directly.
fs: How would you architect a multi-region Azure OpenAI setup for a latency-sensitive production service?

followups.q: How do you handle the 'content_filter' error returned by Azure OpenAI without crashing the user experience?
followups.a: Catch `openai.BadRequestError` and inspect `error.code == 'content_filter'`; surface a sanitized message to the user and log the `content_filter_results` annotation (which tells you which category triggered: hate, self-harm, sexual, violence) to your observability pipeline for policy review.

followups.q: What is 'model fine-tuning' on Azure OpenAI and when does it outperform prompt engineering?
followups.a: Azure OpenAI allows supervised fine-tuning of supported models via the fine-tuning API, uploading JSONL training files and producing a custom deployment. It outperforms prompt engineering when the task requires consistent output format, domain-specific tone, or compression of lengthy few-shot examples into model weights—but it carries retraining cost and model versioning overhead, so it is justified only when latency/token-cost improvements outweigh that overhead.

followups.q: How does Azure OpenAI's 'On Your Data' feature work under the hood?
followups.a: On Your Data injects a retrieval step between the user turn and the model: Azure AI Search performs a vector or hybrid query against your indexed documents, and the top-k chunks are appended as a system context block before the model generates. Internally it uses Azure AI Search as the retrieval backend and can be configured with role-based access so only data the calling user is authorized to see enters the context.

q: Azure AI Foundry (formerly Azure Machine Learning Studio + AI Studio unified) is Microsoft's end-to-end AI development hub. What capabilities does AI Foundry add over raw Azure ML and Azure OpenAI Service, and how does its project/hub model organize multi-team governance?
a: Azure AI Foundry introduces a two-level hierarchy: a Hub (shared infrastructure — compute, connections, policies, shared keys) and Projects (team- or use-case-scoped workspaces that inherit hub resources but maintain isolated artifact stores and access controls). This allows a platform team to govern GPU quotas, approve model deployments, and enforce policy at the hub level while application teams iterate independently in projects. AI Foundry also ships a prompt-flow authoring surface for LLM orchestration DAGs, a model catalog with benchmark comparisons and responsible-AI scorecards, evaluation harnesses (groundedness, relevance, fluency), and first-class connections to Azure OpenAI, Azure AI Search, and external model endpoints. The Foundry SDK (`azure-ai-projects`) unifies project-level operations in one client rather than juggling separate ML and OpenAI SDK clients. Comparing to raw Azure ML, Foundry adds the generative-AI primitives; comparing to Azure OpenAI Service alone, it adds retrieval, evaluation, and deployment orchestration.

fs: How would you use AI Foundry's evaluation harness to gate a prompt-flow deployment in CI/CD?

followups.q: How do Foundry 'connections' differ from storing credentials in environment variables?
followups.a: Connections are managed objects stored in the hub's Azure Key Vault–backed store; they are referenced by name in prompt flows and SDK calls, so credentials never appear in code or pipeline YAML. They also carry type metadata (AzureOpenAI, AzureAISearch, etc.) that lets the SDK auto-configure the right client, and access is governed by hub RBAC rather than secret sharing.

followups.q: What is prompt flow and how does it compare to LangChain for production use on Azure?
followups.a: Prompt flow is a DAG-based authoring and evaluation framework native to Azure AI Foundry; flows are stored as YAML with explicit node types (LLM, Python, prompt). It provides built-in evaluation metrics, batch runs against datasets, and one-click deployment as a managed endpoint. LangChain is more flexible and framework-agnostic but requires you to build your own evaluation and deployment layer; for Azure-centric enterprises, prompt flow reduces operational surface area.

followups.q: How does AI Foundry's model catalog handle gated models like Meta Llama?
followups.a: Gated models require you to accept the publisher's license terms once per subscription via the catalog UI or API; after acceptance, the model is deployable as a managed compute endpoint or serverless API. The catalog records the acceptance event for compliance audit, and hub-level policies can restrict which model families project members are permitted to deploy.

q: Azure AI Search supports both keyword (BM25) and vector search. How do you implement hybrid retrieval with semantic ranking, and what are the trade-offs between pure vector, pure keyword, and hybrid modes for a RAG pipeline?
a: Azure AI Search hybrid search combines a BM25 keyword run and a vector ANN (HNSW) run in parallel, then fuses their ranked lists using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). On top of RRF, enabling the semantic ranker re-scores the top-k fused results using a cross-encoder hosted by Microsoft, producing a captions-and-highlights answer alongside reranked scores. Pure vector excels on paraphrase and conceptual queries but misses exact-match tokens (product codes, error codes); pure keyword nails exact tokens but fails on synonyms and intent variations. Hybrid captures both, and semantic reranking further boosts precision at the cost of added latency (~150–400 ms) and semantic ranker capacity units. For production RAG, a common pattern is hybrid with semantic ranking at query time, chunked documents indexed with `text-embedding-3-large` vectors, and `vectorFilterMode: preFilter` to apply security filters before ANN search (avoiding post-filter precision loss).
